sentences.text: Around '43, '44, I think.
Yeah.
But they found out about the bunker.
We had to go to hide at the farmers.
Because they knew where the bunker was.
The Ukrainians knew.
And that was enough.
And they would tell them where they-- we are, we were afraid to stay there.
In the bunker we stayed for about, maybe four months, three, four months, until they found out about us.
The whole family, we always stay together.
My father, mom, and my brother.
And there were two other families from the village that stayed together with us there.
OK.
My mom's name was Sally.
My father, his name was Samuel.
They called him Sam for short.
And my brother is Bernard, Bernie.
We were-- as family-- of two more families.
But one left.
We were just with another family there.
And they were local-- local people.
They were always living in that village, Ponikwa.
And we stayed with them in the bunker there, I remember.
They were sort of like farmers too.
They were doing farm work.
Jews, but they were doing farm work for-- we were not allowed to own farms there in Europe, in Poland.
So they were doing work for the farmers.
And they got paid and all that.
That's how they make their living.
They remained alive.
They all remained alive.
They were hiding also like we did, at different places.
Because after they found that out, we found this hiding place where my friends were killed, my-- the girl and her brother got killed.
And we found that place, and we stayed there, until that time when they killed.
They knew we were-- we were afraid to stay there any longer.
So we went back into the forest.
My father knew a Polish nobleman there.
He was helping us.
And he told us, you cannot stay in this village anymore.
And he gave us a contact, and we was about like five miles from Ponikwa was another little village.
And it used to be called Wlochy, W-L-O-C-H-Y.
And there was-- there were large forests there.
And they-- he sent us one Jewish guy, he was a local man.
He knew all the-- every angle of the forest, everything.
And he led us into the forest.
And then he got us acquainted with some Ukrainian Polacks.
They were very nice.
In that village, they were all very nice to us.
I think 90% of them were with us.
It's just amazing that we were just so lucky.
And they were sympathetic to us.
And they even brought us food to the forest.
We used to go for food to get food usually.
But sometimes they used to bring it with the horse and buggy.
And they used to unload it into the bunkers.
They helped us build bunkers in the forest.
OK, bunker, they dug a hole in the ground by hand.
They used to come.
We used to work on it, maybe five people, for six people.
A large hole, probably the size of this living room, which is probably 18 by-- what would you say, 20?
Something like that.
And they dug a hole.
They put beams across the top.
They put from the forest, they cut-- it was in the deep forest.
They cut the wood.
They made wood walls, paneled-- like paneled the walls.
And then they on top of it, they put earth and seeded like we call grass.
I don't know.
It was wild stuff there.
So that it was masked.
It was camouflaged.
On top, they put a camouflage.
It was flush, flush with the ground.
So in case, you couldn't see it.
We just had little steps, like going down in the basement.
We had about-- I don't know-- six, seven, eight steps to go down, like down in the basement, to that bunker.
And then the bunker, they put a little plate and a stove.
We had a little stove.
And they used to have potatoes.
We could cook potatoes.
We used to pick mushrooms, and strawberries, I remember in the summertime.
And there was--
Water, there was a-- we used to walk to a little spring, which was about maybe half a mile away.
And we used to take a jug, and we used to bring water back fresh, water to drink in the summertime.
In the wintertime-- we were in the woods like that for 18 months.
From about 1944, I'd say, until the war ended, we were in that bunker there.
And we used to bring-- in the wintertime, we used to melt snow for water.
And we used to bathe, and we used to drink that water.
That's what we did.
And all summer, we used to pick little mushrooms.
I remember, dry them up for the winter.
So we had something to eat in the wintertime.
And they used to bring us potatoes and stuff like that.
Really, we were hungry.
But I wouldn't say we suffered real hunger like they did in the camps.
So I was lucky.
We were lucky in that respect.
And by the way, something worthwhile to mention, there were about eight or 10 Jewish families spread out, and all in the same type of bunker, bunkers like we had.
And we used to visit them.
We used to go visit them there.
And all survived but two people.
And that was not because they were Jews.
That was a personal thing.
I remember the woman fell in love with one of the guys that was married.
And his wife was jealous.
And she sent some guys.
And they killed her and her son.
And then her father, he's alive.
He remained alive.
Maybe he's still alive.
Yeah, Jewish, all Jewish, yeah.
And he stayed with us for a while.
And he knew all the people there.
He was a very, very nice.
We was all Jewish.
There were all Jewish families there.
What happened after the war, we escaped.
That's another thing.
We were-- the Russians liberated us in '45, around '45.
And we didn't want to stay with the Russians.
So illegally somehow, we came to Austria.
There was Rothschild's hospital in Russia-- in Austria, Vienna.
That was a culminating point for the Jews from all over, remained-- the Jews that remained after the war.
And we got there with the idea to go to Germany, in order to go to the United States or Israel, one or the other.
So that was our plan.
We stayed there for a few months.
And later we got transported to Munich.
And we lived in Munich from about '45 till '49, '46 maybe close-- maybe a little over three years we were in Munich.
Go ahead.
You see, as far as my memory goes, it must have been a year and a few months, or maybe a year and a half, something like that.
Nothing at all.
We did not hear.
We had no news.
We just heard that the Jews were being killed from the surrounding villages.
They told us that they heard that-- they didn't know about it.
They just heard that Jews are being killed.
No.
We didn't know the gas chambers.
We didn't know about the camps, and didn't know anything-- we didn't know how, what.
We thought that they were being killed in the woods like they did before.
They were shot, and shooting Jews in the woods.
But they didn't do it like that we found out later.
Yeah.
We anticipated a lot of bad things, but not that bad.
Because during the first war, the 1914 war, the Germans were very good to the Jews.
And the Jews had it very good in Germany.
You know that.
They had high positions, high posts.
What happened is in 1920s, the '20s, when Hitler got into power.
And he poisoned a lot of the world that maybe where they had that in them, but they didn't show it.
Our-- my feelings about Germany when the war started, even before the war started, I heard German, Hitler talk on the radio, screaming.
I didn't know what he was talking about.
He was screaming so bad, badly.
You probably heard those speeches that he made.
And I-- he sounded like-- like a crazy man to me.
And I was scared of him a lot.
Because they were against the Jews.
We knew that Nazis were against the Jews.
They-- my-- I didn't understand his speeches.
He spoke in German.
But my parents understood it.
I didn't know German then.
And they were talking at the dinner table, talking what are the Germans are against the Jews.
There were not mass killings at that time.
But that they were against, and I was very scared of Hitler.
I mean I was-- I remember the first bombs fell, when I knew he was coming.
I didn't know what was going to happen.
I was scared.
Yeah.
Oh, yes, all around us.
We almost got killed, and oh, there were bombs all around us.
Some were killed.
I didn't know their names.
I don't remember their names.
My relatives, my-- I had three aunts, I had my grandmother.
They were all, remained alive.
We had a little bunker.
We were under the basement stairs when the bombs fell, and we survived somehow.
Do you mean in the forest in the bunker?
We had-- they also made us like cots from wood and straw.
We laid on straw.
We slept on the straw.
Only five of us, in one-- my bunker.
At first it was four, just our family.
And then we took this guy that I told you, his wife and son was killed.
And he came to us, and we took him in.
And we barely made it there.
But we stayed together with him.
So there were five of us.
Itzak, Isaac something.
Itzak, it was a Jewish name.
I don't remember his last name.
He was in New York.
Yeah, I heard from him later.
I heard that he was alive in New York.
And he was doing very well.
He was a businessman.
And he was sharp.
He was doing pretty good after the war.
OK.
What happened is we had a leader in the-- I told you there was about eight or so families in there.
One was our leader like.
He was a very military man.
He had a gun with him, a short-- a sort of shotgun.
A Jew, yeah.
And he was-- we made him our leader.
Whatever he said, we did.
We followed him.
And like we used to go at night, we had to go to get food.
So he used to go with us.
Maybe five or six of us went out of the forest, which was about a five mile walk.
We walked outside the forest.
We dug some potatoes out from the farmers there and then brought them back.
We brought about 20 pounds or 30 pounds.
We hauling back to the forest.
And that's the way we may-- you could survive.
So one day, I remember, he sends us away quickly.
He said, if you want to get out alive, get out now.
Because the Russians just came.
And we don't know if the Germans are going to come back soon.
So we left everything, and we left, we ran.
We ran, and it was about like five or six miles until we got out of the forest.
It was the front line there.
I remember shootings all over, just it was a mess.
We even went through machine guns, and then cannons, and everything else.
And we saw people, they buried the soldiers right there on the spot.
There was no place to bury.
They buried them there.
Killed and blood.
We ran through it, and we went back.
I was what?
I was about-- I was not so young then.
I was about 17 then in 1945.
Somehow we got through the front lines.
And we went a little bit in the back.
And there were soldiers all over, Russian soldiers everywhere.
And they fed us.
They gave us some of their rations.
And I remember how-- and we kept on walking away from the front line.
East?
We were walking.
Let me get my bearings.
No.
We were walking away from the front line.
Wait a minute.
No, we were going west.
Wait a minute.
I'm getting confused here now.
Just-- just a minute.
Well, the Russians occupied.
They came and occupied us.
We were going with the Russians, we're actually going-- the front line was-- was west of us.
We were walking-- we were going with them in file with the Russians.
They took us on a truck.
I remember, and they took us to a little city called Krzemieniec.
That's hard to pronounce, Krzemieniec.
And we stayed, and my dad got a job there too, right, with the Russians.
So again, he got into the food business again.
He got a job there.
That's another story.
Bombed out houses, I remember.
We used to stop, when the truck last left us, we just-- houses were bombed out.
We went there, and we just slept there through the night.
Or sometimes--
No, no, just one night, or outside doors.
We stayed outdoors.
And we walked most of the time.
And sometimes, like I said, the-- the military truck would pick us up and take us a few miles, and drop us off.
And that's the way we went away from the-- we went-- we followed the Russians there.
They were treating us very well then, very, very good.
They were treating us fair.
They knew we were Jewish, and they treated us very nicely.
They didn't know themselves, until they got in there to Germany, deep Germany.
I don't think anybody knew much.
Americans didn't knew much-- at that-- know much as far as I remember reading books about it, until they got into the concentration camps, and saw what was happening.
Generally, yeah, we knew they were killing Jews.
But we didn't know how or what--
To what extent, we didn't know anything about that, no.
I had a grandma, and my grandpa died just before the war, and my paternal and maternal grandma.
I remember my mom's mother, I remember.
My father's mom I vaguely remember.
No.
They-- they were killed.
All, everybody except our family, everybody was killed.
The only survivor was my uncle, younger uncle.
And he ran away to Hungary, and he survived it.
He just passed away last year.
Oh, yeah.
Everybody was killed, everybody, my aunts, my cousins, the whole-- there's not one except my--
We don't know.
We'll never know.
They was in the ghetto in Brody.
They all lived in Brody.
And that's all we know.
There was a ghetto in Brody, and what happened after that, we went to that village.
We never heard from them again.
What happened to them, we don't know.
Hiding in the woods, part of the time.
Part of the time we were hiding with farmers.
We were in hiding places.
Well, we paid them.
We had some-- a little money left yet, whatever we had left.
And then at the end, the money ran out.
So we were-- they were sympathetic.
And they, like I said, that Polish he was like a knight, what you call him that-- I can't remember.
He was from the no-- nobility, Polish nobility.
He was our friend.
He was a wonderful guy, and he helped us a lot.
He hated the Germans.
Most Polacks hated the Germans.
But they also disliked the Jews.
That was a problem.
And Ukrainians, they were just terrible.
They were very bad.
Because they wanted the one thing the Germans promised them, independent Ukraine.
They wanted independent Ukraine.
And they were just not very-- most of them in general, generally speaking, were not very well educated.
And they just, they were anti-Semitic.
The Polacks were more refined.
I don't have much against the Polacks, really.
Except they were so anti-Semitic too, some of them.
But they were nice to us.
The Ukrainians, I can't say the same thing.
They were very bad, anti-Semitic and bad, very few exceptions.
That day when my friends were killed.
That was about the worst thing that-- I still have nightmares.
This will never end, as long as I live when that scream and they shot-- and they shot her, my-- she was going to school with me to [NON-ENGLISH], I told you, [NON-ENGLISH].
She was killed, and her father, and her brother.
Her mother was on the Aryan papers.
And she was somewhere else.
She didn't know about it till later.
That's probably one of the-- there were many bad days.
But this is something that's in my mind until today.
I don't think I'll ever forget that.
I never did try to do that, never did, no.
My mom had a chance to be on Aryan papers.
But she didn't want to leave us.
She said if we die, we die together.
And that's why we stayed as a family.
We stayed together.
She didn't want to leave us.
She could have, with my brother she could have left, as that nobleman's relative.
He wanted to make her a cousin of his and give her papers.
But she didn't want to leave us.
Because she had to live in freedom.
We had to be in hiding.
She would be free as an Aryan.
Oh, no, no.
We were too Semitic.
We looked Jewish, and no.
She didn't look too Jewish.
You could see her pictures probably here.
You can see that she didn't.
No, she didn't look too Jewish.
She was like reddish hair.
Oh, ho, ho.
When I was younger, I wish I wouldn't have been Jewish.
You know, things like that go through your mind deep in your heart.
I loved the Jewish life.
I enjoyed it so much.
But there was a revolution in me, inside of me.
If I wasn't Jewish, I would be free.
I wouldn't have to be in hiding.
I wouldn't be afraid.
And the-- the Gentiles were not afraid of anything.
And I was afraid, scared.
I mean I don't think it's human nature only.
I-- I would like to be there a transformation right there.
Not all the time, but the memories from the Jewish life are so rich and so wonderful, it was like a-- inside like, what do you want to be?
Do you-- do you want to have freedom?
You don't want to be Jewish, and then you still want to be Jewish.
So you don't know.
It's just conflict, very difficult conflict.
You wonder-- I wondered about God.
I did have questions in me.
I didn't know.
[SOBBING] Is this the God I was praying to?
Is this what-- I just couldn't understand.
I really-- I couldn't understand it, all that killings and other-- I just could not understand it really.
And I wanted to live so badly.
I mean when you are young like that, your life is so much more precious than when you get older.
You do want to live.
You didn't live yet.
You want to-- you have a life in front of you.
The life is so precious then.
When you get older, it's still everybody wants to live.
But it's not-- not just like before.
Like life is so very precious then when you're young, when you're in the teens.
Oh, God, this emotion.
I don't know.
I try to compose myself.
At the beginning when they said that I had hatred, and that hatred almost destroyed me.
I don't have hatred.
I just have pity for those people, what they did to us, really more than hatred.
And I don't want to hate anybody.
I don't want to.
Because it will kill me.
Hatred will kill me.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Yeah.
In 1944, well, we were in the bunker then.
We were in the forest.
We were hiding in the bunker.
That's what happened--
After the woods, like I told you, we were following the Russians.
And we went to a place called Krzemieniec.
I remember we lived there for a few months.
And then from there, we went-- I remember passing through Warsaw, and going to the West.
We went to Warsaw.
We went to Czechoslovakia.
I remember being in Bratislava after the war.
That was after the war already.
Bratislava, and then Vienna, to that Rothschild Hospital.
That's all.
Nothing-- nothing else really happened that's worth telling.
They were treating us well.
They were not treating us badly.
The Russians, after the war, yeah.
I was in a bunker there, in that forest, when they told us that we should leave right now.
Because the Russians occupied there, and they don't know when the Germans are going to be back.
They might come back.
So we ran away.
Right away, we just ran with the Russians there.
And that's what exactly--
No, no, no.
It wasn't over.
It was the-- I ran into the front lines.
I told you that.
Remember? 1944, '45, there was still a war going on.
Oh, yeah.
At that time we were-- when the war ended, I-- we went to Krzemieniec, that town.
That was in Poland, too, yet.
And when the war ended, after that then we traveled west.
Like we went to Warsaw, Czechoslovakia.
Czechoslovakia is like south.
We went by train mostly, mostly by train.
I don't know the details really.
My parents arranged things.
I don't know all the details about it.
Me and my brother, and my mom, my dad, we all get together.
That's all I remember.
I don't remember how, why.
I remember from Vienna, then the Bricha came.
The-- from Israel, they were helping us getting to Germany.
Because we were not allowed to go out.
But we went to Germany.
And that's-- that's all I can tell you about it.
Nothing eventful happened.
From Poland to Czechoslovakia, from Czechoslovakia we went to Austria, Vienna.
From Vienna we went to Munich.
Actually, we went to Nuremberg, Furth to Nuremberg, where Kissinger is from there.
Remember, he is from Furth, that city.
And we went there because there was a DP camp, displaced persons camp.
In Furth.
So we went to that displaced persons camp.
Well, I tell you, my mom and my brother lived there from right after the war until '49, '49, maybe three or four years.
Yeah, a few years.
Me?
I stayed there for about a year or two, and then I went to school in Munich.
So my dad worked in Munich for AJDC.
He was in uniform, and he was working for the food distribution.
And he was the manager, like, of the food distribution for the Jewish people.
And that was all sponsored by the Americans, you know, AJDC.
And I went to school there.
I went to the [NON-ENGLISH] for one year.
That's the Technical Institute of Munich.
When I think about it now, I don't how I could be with the Germans there and go to the same school.
But everything was-- after the war you were like in a daze.
You didn't know what was happening to you.
And you lived with the Germans.
And there were other Jewish students that went, survivors that went to school.
Average German person, I would say, generally speaking, they were selfish people.
This is my opinion now.
They would-- they didn't mistreat us after the war, of course.
We lived there.
But they didn't like us.
I could feel it.
They didn't like us.
And somehow we stayed with them, and I don't know.
And I couldn't do it now.
I couldn't stay among them now.
I'd get a nervous breakdown or something.
At that time it was an improvement.
Because right after the war, after the Nazis, and you came.
They treated you better.
You were among Jewish people.
A lot of them were Jewish people in Munich.
And there was a Jewish Center.
And I had my friends.
There was about 10, about 12 Jewish fellows that went to school with me there.
And I started.
I got my start of college there, and took engineering, at [NON-ENGLISH].
Yes.
I had a tutor that was tutoring me in math, and he was a German.
And I used to go to his house.
He said, we could have won the war.
But things got so-- and he was so arrogant to even then, young man.
And I said, I don't know politics.
And I don't want to discuss politics.
I came to study math here.
If you want to-- we gave him food for that.
My dad had food.
And food was a very great commodity.
And he tutored me in math.
So I said, I really don't want to discuss politics.
And you know, I just hated this guy at that time.
I hated him.
OK.
OK.
